speaker: interviewer
text: Interview with Helen Granek, tape number three. Helen, we were talking about the camp in Czestochowa. Could you continue telling me that this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Now as I said, at that stage, there were 500 men and the-- and my uncle and my aunt and I. My uncle straight away started pressing the Germans to construct some sort of a hospital and infirmary so that people could be treated properly. The name of the camp was Warta Arbeitslager, which is a working camp. It was actually an ammunition factory. And the 500 men from the ghetto would go into this factory every morning and work. And there were Polish people who would come from the outside also to work in this camp. When my aunt and uncle were searched, when we came to the previous camp, my aunt was able-- they-- all their precious belong-- even her wedding ring was taken off her finger, but she managed to hide a little diamond. She actually sewed it. The dress which she was wearing, which she still had from home, had-- has four pockets with little bows on the pockets. So she was able to sew inside the, the little bow, the diamond. But when they knew that they would go through a search going out of the camp going into the new camp, my auntie took the diamond out and my uncle swallowed it. He actually, actually swallowed it on two separate occasions. And he was lucky later to be able to go to the toilet and, and to find it. When they go up to the camp in Czestochowa, they were able to sell this diamond to one of the Poles who came in every day. And for the remainder of our stay in the camp, we were able to buy a little bit of extra bread, a little bit of extra nourishment, which the Poles brought, brought in as their lunches. And they also used to bring in their lunches wrapped in newspapers, which they sold to my uncle, who really wanted to know what was going on in the war. Although the papers were printed in German, and it was a very one-sided sort of reporting, but at least he could read between the lines. Because-- but if you were caught with a newspaper you could be shot. So he was a terrific risk taker, but he just had this obsession that he wanted to know what was going on. Now eventually, the population of this camp grew to 4,000. And there were a lot of women and some children. And again, the same process started. One day a search party came to take all the children. I was hidden under a mattress. And fortunately, they didn't find me. So that's another miracle of my survival. The Germans eventually-- or the Jewish people in the ghetto, in the camp, built a small hospital as surgery. And behind the surgery a tiny little room where the, the people that had arrived in the meantime, there were a few other doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the doctors and their families slept and lived in this tiny little room. So we actually didn't have to live in the general barrack. We had our own quarters, which was-- they were cramped and they were dirty and everything, but at least was our quarters. And that's how we, we lived till, till our liberation. What did you do in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I helped-- I helped a little bit in the surgery. I probably cleaned and did things like that. Another risk that my uncle took, and he used to go every now and again outside into the town with a German guard or a Lithuanian guard to buy medication. And he used to give this-- the guard a few-- a bit of money to go buy himself a drink. So he-- my uncle actually enrolled under an assumed name in a library. And he used to bring us books smuggled in between the medical supplies. He could've been shot for that, but he wanted me to go on with-- to learn reading, to be able to read books. So my education, as best as could be, was continued in the camp. My uncle used to give me maths lessons with mixed results. Because I wasn't good at maths. And he used to sit and read these books with me. So it was really-- when I think back, he could have been shot so many times. And yet he, he survived. It's just something unbelievable. The, the German commandant of the ghetto was a man called Bartenschlager. And he was just-- he was a true, true SS man. He could shoot anybody in such cold blood that it was just, just horrible to think of the things that he had done. There was a Jewish-- a German Jew who was in charge of our camp. He was in an inmate like we were, but he was a Nazi collaborator and a Nazi sympathizer. He was an absolutely horrible, corrupted man. And he used to steal from supplies. He did some pretty horrible things. And I believe that after the war, he was caught and, and executed, which is exactly what he deserved. There was another man in the ghetto called Mr. Ziskind who looked after provisions. And as the war went on, he became more and more corrupt. He used to take-- he wouldn't give us food. He used to take food and sell it on the black market because he was in charge of the food stores. So unfortunately, even among our own people, there was some pretty horrible ones. There were acts of heroism. There were people in the barracks who used to look after young girl. There were a lot of young girls, teenage girls or girls in there twenties. And the older women looked after them, and same with the men. And it was just unbelievable some of the acts of heroism have one covered for another case, in case one would be punished. There was some fairly orthodox people in the camps. And I don't know how because nobody had calendars, they used to work out with the Jewish festivals were. And to the extend that if you didn't eat bread, the other provisions were so meager, you really couldn't survive. But they used to work it out when it was Pesach. And they used to swap bread for soup. So they used to eat the soup and not eat bread for a week. And I think that in itself was heroic, because we were so undernourished and sickness was so rife that they could have died from malnutrition. But they just wouldn't eat bread over Pesach. My uncle-- as I said, my uncle used to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] buy newspapers, and my aunt used to stand guard. If the Germans came, she used to give them a sign to hide the newspapers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do any of the inmates stand out? Did you make friends with anyone in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, we actually did. There was a nursing sister. She'd been a nurse in Czestochowa for many years. She became a very close friend, and we spent time with her. She lived with us after the war. And she was-- we actually brought her out here, and she lived here for many years before she died. And the man whom she married after the war was an orderly, medical orderly in the hospital. And she was the nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris. And there were-- there were some other people in the barracks. There were people here still in Melbourne that we were in camps with. And it was just-- the, the bond was just fantastic because we'd been through so much together that there was a sort of a lasting bond between us. My uncle was head of the hospital. He was also sort of an unofficial leader of the ghetto because the real leader was so corrupt. My uncle was so good and so helpful that the people came to him not only for medical advice, but just for moral support, I think, for a kind word, which he was always able to give to everybody. And that extended after the liberation as well because people were just so alone. At least we were still a family unit. People were just so alone, so they came just for a kind word.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your survival strategies while you lived in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to keep on living. Not to give in.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think just hope that one day it would be over. We, we really didn't know, not just from one day to another, but from one minute to another. Could have been shot, could get some terrible illness, could just die. It was-- it was pretty horrible, very frightening, pretty horrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Towards the end of the war, did you have any news of what was happening in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They knew-- well, actually, we knew at what stage America entered the war. We knew that's the Russian invasion had fail-- the German invasion of Russia had failed. We knew that the Allies were advancing on all fronts. Just exactly how much, we didn't know. Then one day we could hear bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the bombing?

speaker: interviewee
text: The bombing was on the outskirts of Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see it? Did you see the airplanes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Possibly, I don't know. I can just remember the roar of the bombs. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My uncle was called in December 1944, actually New Year's Eve 1944, my uncle was called to the German head of the camp. His wife was expecting a baby. And I don't know why, but she wanted this Jewish doctor to deliver her baby. Now she had one child, and she'd had difficulty with her pregnancy. And my uncle was asked to deliver the baby. Now if he would have-- if something would have happened to that baby, my uncle would have been shot. But fortunately, he delivered them a healthy child. And as a reward, he was given a bottle of some drink and a cake. And it was New Year's Eve, 1944. He came back with these two things. And the little medical community in the room next to the hospital had a little New Year's Eve celebration. And we wished ourselves a quick delivery from all this. But we didn't know that 15 days later we would be liberated. Now on the eve of the 16th of January, you could hear rumbling. Oh yes, about a-- we also knew things were not good because 800 able-bodied men from the camp were rounded up and taken to Germany. And this is what they used to do as things closed in. They took the more able-bodied ones into Germany so they could still work for the Reich. And it was January. It was in the middle of winter, and we heard later that not many of these people survived. So they actually survived most of the war, and were killed just at the end, which was very tragic. The night of the 15th of January, all of a sudden, there was silence. The rumbling was stopping and there was silence in the camp. And everybody was up. Nobody could go to sleep that night. I think there was a feeling that something was about to happen. And my uncle then went to this little guard house where all the guards used to be, guarding the camp. And he realized that the Germans had gone. And that only a couple of Lithuanian or Ukrainian or something guards were left. He disarmed them, and he actually walked back into the camp with a gun. And we knew we were liberated. That was in the early hours of the 16th of January, 1945. So that for us was the end of the war, but it really wasn't the end of suffering. People went crazy. They started to storm the food stores. My uncle tried, sometimes in vain, because he was worried because we had starved for so long, that if people started eating, they would get sick. And some of them did. Cause we had no-- very little to eat the last few years. But the Russians had arrived, and we were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the Russians coming to the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they were friendly. They were the victors. They were the liberating, liberating army. They were pretty wild, some of them. They drank a lot. They, they robbed wherever they could. But they were our liberators, so, you know, always have that special feeling for the Red Army, whether you believe in their political leanings or not. But they were our liberators. They were our saviours. So that was the end of, of this horrible time. But [SIGHS] the sadness and the tragedy was still to come because as war finished through the Red Cross, through all different agencies, we heard that none of the rest of-- the rest of our family had perished. So it was very, very sad. I was very lucky. I was with two wonderful people who were loving, who looked after me. I must've been a very, very lonely person, even though I was loved because I had lost my parents. And that's been with me more so lately than before. Because I just can't help feeling upset that my parents never lived to see me grow beyond eight years. They didn't see me grow up. They didn't see me educated. They didn't see me get married. They never knew that they had grandchildren. They never had the pleasure of loving grandchildren, and enjoying the 50-odd years that I have enjoyed living in Australia. So it's very sad. And my grandmother-- she had two children in Australia, two in Poland, and one in Israel. So her family was dispersed. And she must've been only in her 60s when she was taken to Treblinka. Or probably a bit more in her 70s. It was very sad when people were just like lost souls when they were liberated. There was joy, and there was great sadness and great tragedy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went out and you just requisitioned a flat from the Poles. A lot of the nicer flats were empty because they'd been occupied by the Germans. So you just walked in and you squatted, and that was it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My uncle started working in a hospital. I was sent to school. That was my first lot of formal schooling since my kindergarten days. I went to sixth grade because I was already 11 at that stage. And we somehow settled into a more or less normal life. The anti-Semitism in Poland was just terrible. The Poles resented that any Jew had survived. Oh, you survived! And you look so well! And you're wearing a hat! Like you had to apologize for, for still being alive. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We kept in touch with some of the inmates from the-- from the camp who also settled in Czestochowa after the war. A lot of people came for advice, for a meal, for a kind word. And then one-- and then we made contact with my family here in Australia. Before we knew it, we had permits to come here. And the decision wasn't easy because my uncle knew that he wouldn't be able to practice as a doctor in Australia. But then a pogrom took place in one of the city's not far from Czestochowa. Actually, a few months ago there was a 50 year commemoration of this pogrom. And that pogrom made my uncle and aunt decide that it was time to leave. That there was no future for us. Because not only had the Poles organized this pogrom, but to the Russians also very quietly supported it. So between the Russians and the Poles, we were really in a very precarious situation. And my uncle was frightened to go out on, on night calls because if somebody asked him to come to look to a sick person, it could've been a hoax. So he was really not safe working in Poland. So in July 1946, we packed up, and we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was very excited about going to a new country. And I thought that everybody in this country was rich and happy. It seemed like a-- like a paradise. My-- I was doing well at school, but the anti-Semitism was dreadful. Actually, that was my first proper contact with Polish anti-Semites on a daily basis. I had one girlfriend. And she and I really got on very well. And one day-- she used to come over to my place.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. One day she invited me to her place. And she lived with her mother and her grandfather. And the next day she came crying to me. She said, I'll play with you at school, but I'm not allowed to ask you to my place, and I'm not allowed to come over to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was my life at school. As I said, I did well academically, but socially it was just dreadful. So we were very glad that we were able to leave Poland. We traveled by train from Czestochowa to Prague, from Prague to Paris. Took days.

speaker: interviewee
text: We actually stopped in Prague for two days, which was beautiful. It was a lovely city. And my uncle had a sister living in Paris who survived the war. So we stayed with her for about six to eight weeks. And then we went to Marseilles, and there was a ship waiting for us. There were hundreds and hundreds of Jewish migrants. The boat was sub, substandard. Our conditions were dreadful. But we were going away from-- It wasn't just escaping Poland. The whole of Europe was like a graveyard. And we were just glad to, to leave that all behind. We would have perhaps preferred to go to Palestine, but under the Bridgeman Mandate, that wasn't possible. So we settled for Australia. And we arrived in Australia. It was a terrible journey. I was sick the whole way. We traveled for 10 weeks through all the French colonies in the Caribbean. It was an interesting journey, but it was-- we had very little to eat. Because the Russians-- the French didn't have-- my, my family in Australia paid for first class tickets. And we were about sixth or seventh class. But on the 25th of November 1946, I got up in the morning, and there was Sydney Harbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that like, seeing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wonderful. It was 5 o'clock in the morning, I was out on the deck. It was just beautiful. It was the most beautiful sight I had ever seen.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop the tape there.
